storySections.title: Basic Information
storySections.content:
"Welcome to Lady Daffodil's Exchange Shop. We barter goods and collect heads. Our rates are perfectly fair—fair to all, no deception."

Amidst the swirling gray mist, the woman gently tapped the ash from her antique wooden pipe. Beneath the swaying brim of her hat, a flickering smile hovered at the corners of her lips, laced with ambiguity.

storySections.title: Story: I
storySections.content:
Daffodil sold herself to the Silver Core Research Laboratory of Mythag University at a staggering price.

Before that, the laboratory head Doll had repeatedly expressed strong dissatisfaction with the one-way communications that only Daffodil could initiate.

But only Daffodil and a certain deceased high-ranking member of Mythag University know how such a "one-way communication" was established through a dangerous transaction.

storySections.title: Story: II
storySections.content:
Daffodil's whereabouts at Mythag University are nothing short of ghostly. After all, she possesses taboo knowledge of interdimensional travel, allowing her to come and go as she pleases, entirely unfettered by Black Pool's Silver Core Hub.

She routinely deals in mysterious instruments and materials of dubious origin, and sometimes assists other Awakeners in briefly evading the oversight of Mythag University.

Later, through negotiations, she opened her own "Worry-Relief Shop" in a disused room beside the infirmary on campus.

Of course, all goods for sale must first be registered and vetted by Mythag University.

storySections.title: Story: III
storySections.content:
On a street corner in a port city sat a tiny exchange shop, run by a proprietor skilled in handling mysterious events.

Not long after the flood, Daffodil—who was not yet known as Daffodil at the time—arrived near St. Peidro. Soon after her arrival, victims of the "mysterious head disappearance" began to surface throughout the harbor city.

The owner investigated, tracked it down, and after exhausting every measure to subdue it, struck a bargain with it. The shopkeeper promised it her own head upon her death, but until then, it must serve as her assistant and could no longer hunt at will.

And so the creature, after a thousand years of wandering, began to work and live by her side.

It had once immersed itself in the memories of countless heads, tasting both fleeting joy and festering resentment. But this absurd happiness was merely a pastime to while away its prolonged existence, masking the profound, drifting loneliness.

Only while accompanying the shopkeeper did it experience genuine friendship for the very first time. It realized that infinite heads offered nothing but a hollow comfort; only true human connection could fill its perpetual void.

However, this brief happiness lasted less than ten years. The shopkeeper, who had never once set aside her work, passed away unexpectedly.

And so it severed her head, inheriting her appearance, shop, and name, becoming "Daffodil."

From that day forward, Daffodil embarked on a journey with her little exchange shop. She continues to claim heads as payment from those who seek her help, keeping them as private collectibles.

But she never hunted at will again.

Never again.

storySections.title: Story: IV
storySections.content:
In the gray, viscous Sea of Primordial Essence, emptiness prevails; the notions shared by travelers crossing dimensions remain confined to abstract descriptions and imagination.

It wants to confirm with its own eyes the "places of human yearning," to witness the magnificent wonders of the mortal world, and to experience the bittersweet nuances of human love and loss.

Yet above all, it desires a specific person, someone to cherish, someone to trust.

A fair trade means equality, detachment, and accounts settled—not a fraction more is paid, nor a fraction less taken. But it yearns for something different—an unconditional trust to bridge the divide, to break through cautious probe, to thaw the lingering ice.

It had found that, then lost it. But it would find it again.
